recent_candidates.title: Iranian ballistic missile operations: 2024–2026
recent_candidates.excerpt: No Images? Click here https://fdd-new.cmail19.com/t/r-e-tutytix-oophjwr-e/ July 2, 2026 | FDD's Long War Journal Iranian ballistic missile operations: 2024–2026 [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/07/02/iranian-ballistic-missile-operations-2024-2026/] Behnam Ben Taleblu [https://www.fdd.org/team/behnam-ben-taleblu/] | Iran Program Senior Director and Senior Fellow Missiles produced by Iran's armed forces on display on February 11, 2026 in Tehran, Iran. (Majid Saeedi/Getty Images) Over the past two years, the Islamic Republic has engaged in at least 15 distinct ballistic missile operations from its territory against foreign targets. These attacks, starting in January 2024, ended a one-year pause (2023) in known ballistic missile attacks from Iranian territory. However, the resumption of attacks in 2024, as the Middle East became engulfed in a series of wars and widening cycles of violence after the October 7, 2023, terrorist attacks by Hamas against Israel, continues a trend of increasing Iranian missile operations in the region from 2017–2022. Utilizing open-source information, including English, Persian, and Arabic language government and (traditional and social) media reporting, F

recent_candidates.excerpt: No Images? Click here https://fdd-new.cmail20.com/t/r-e-tutytljd-oophjwr-tr/ July 2, 2026 | The Christian Post Is Iran becoming a post-Islamic society? [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/07/02/is-iran-becoming-a-post-islamic-society/] Mariam Wahba [https://www.fdd.org/team/mariam-wahba/] | Research Analyst Angelo Schiciano | Intern (Kaveh Kazemi/Getty Images Washington and Tehran signed a memorandum of understanding that has not stopped the exchange of fire in the Gulf but has already produced a renewed [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/06/24/the-deal-with-tehran-comes-at-the-expense-of-iranians/] and more aggressive repression campaign inside Iran. This crackdown can be understood as the regime’s response to Iranians who had begun to imagine what their country would look like without it. Since its founding in 1979, the Islamic Republic’s legitimacy has rested on the claim that it governs in the name of Islam. “In Islam, the legislative power and competence to establish laws belong exclusively to God Almighty,” Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini declared [http://library.snls.org.sz/archive/doc/wikipedia/wikipedia-terodump-0.1/tero-dump/wikipedia/ay/Ayatollah_Khomeini.html#:~:text=%22In%2

recent_candidates.title: A Parliament Without Real Opposition in Syria
recent_candidates.excerpt: No Images? Click here https://fdd-new.cmail19.com/t/r-e-tutyjtul-oophjwr-tt/ July 2, 2026 | Policy Brief A Parliament Without Real Opposition in Syria [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/07/02/a-parliament-without-real-opposition-in-syria/] Ahmad Sharawi [https://www.fdd.org/team/ahmad-sharawi/] | Senior Research Analyst The Syrian parliament building in Damascus on July 1, 2026. (LOUAI BESHARA/AFP via Getty Images) A year and a half after the overthrow of former dictator Bashar al-Assad’s regime, Syria’s new parliament is finally taking shape. On July 1, Syrian President Ahmad al-Sharaa appointed [https://www.reuters.com/world/middle-east/syrias-sharaa-appoints-lawmakers-paving-way-new-parliament-convene-2026-07-01/] 70 lawmakers, one-third of the chamber, following indirect “elections” held between October 2025 and May 2026 to fill the remaining two-thirds of the seats. Rather than allowing citizens to vote directly for their representatives, Syria’s constitutional declaration — effectively its interim constitution — created a layered selection process controlled from the top. The Higher Committee for Parliamentary Elections (HCPE), a body established by Sharaa, appointed election

recent_candidates.title: What % of the Top Quarter of Columbia Law Students Are Black?
recent_candidates.excerpt: View this post on the web at https://www.stevesailer.net/p/what-of-the-top-quarter-of-columbia As you’ll recall, way back in the Cancel Culture Dark Ages of the 2010s, University of Pennsylvania Law School professor Amy Wax got in big trouble for telling [ https://substack.com/redirect/05a01dda-c36b-4df4-b9d6-0123a4f63bd5?j=eyJ1IjoiMmpwdzcifQ.3xfUjhbxmXCecLGnlb_rhBXs-3STnTomTjes1rmBMMI ] economist Glenn Loury: “Here’s a very inconvenient fact, Glenn: I don’t think I’ve ever seen a black student graduate in the top quarter of the class, and rarely, rarely, in the top half,” said Ms. Wax. “I can think of one or two students who scored in the top half of my required first-year course.” Everybody was shocked, shocked [ https://substack.com/redirect/fc004404-2bf9-491e-8fb3-067d1bc0c8cc?j=eyJ1IjoiMmpwdzcifQ.3xfUjhbxmXCecLGnlb_rhBXs-3STnTomTjes1rmBMMI ] by this observation. Much of the Establishment was outraged at Professor Wax for obviously lying, and the rest of the Establishment was outraged at her for telling the truth. Sander on the fall of Viktor Orbán, Gabriel Winslow-Yost on George Saunders’s climate change novel, Omer Bartov on the Nazi’s true enemy, Susan Rubin Suleiman on André Breton’s Nadja, Jed Perl on fashion’s takeover of the Met, David Quammen on the “dark mystery” of genetic mutations, Julia Preston on El Paso, Bryce Covert on Joe Manchin, poems by Jim Johnstone and Laura Kolbe, and much more. Brenda Wineapple [https://www.nybooks.com/articles/2026/07/23/an-uncertain-triumphalism-1876-centennial-exhibition/] An Uncertain Triumphalism [https://www.nybooks.com/articles/2026/07/23/an-uncertain-triumphalism-1876-centennial-exhibition/] America’s centennial in 1876 was celebrated with a grand exhibition that projected an image of national unity and inventiveness in the anxious aftermath of civil war and recession. recent_candidates.title: US250☆TNC: Celebrate the legacy of America's founding with us
recent_candidates.excerpt: View in browser (https://mailchi.mp/newcriterion.com/spring-donation-campaign-2026-email-7?e=e4f33bda35) https://newcriterion.com/donate/?utm_source=The+New+Criterion+Subscribers&utm_campaign=27443f1c95-Donation_CAMPAIGN_2019_fall_Email_1_COPY_01&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_f42f7adca5-27443f1c95-479650245 Dear reader, Two hundred fifty years ago today, on July 2, 1776, George Washington issued a general order to his troops on the eve of the New York campaign. The British fleet—the largest armada ever seen in North America—had begun disembarking redcoat soldiers on Staten Island. Sensing that he stood at one of the great turning points of history, Washington steeled the hearts of his assembled soldiers: “The time is now near at hand which must probably determine whether Americans are to be freemen or slaves; whether they are to have any property they can call their own; whether their houses and farms are to be pillaged and destroyed, and themselves consigned to a state of wretchedness from which no human efforts will deliver them. . . . Let us therefore animate and encourage each other, and shew the whole world, that a Freeman contending for Liberty on his own ground is superior to

Click here https://fdd-new.cmail19.com/t/r-e-tutruilt-oophjwr-s/ July 2, 2026 | Memo Meet the 13 Palestinians Tasked With Governing Gaza After Hamas [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/07/02/meet-the-13-palestinians-tasked-with-governing-gaza-after-hamas/] David Daoud [https://www.fdd.org/team/david-daoud/] | Senior Fellow Ahmad Sharawi [https://www.fdd.org/team/ahmad-sharawi/] | Senior Research Analyst (Illustration by Daniel Ackerman/FDD) View Full Memo [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/07/02/meet-the-13-palestinians-tasked-with-governing-gaza-after-hamas/] Hamas refuses to disarm, but following two years of war with Israel, it may soon cede its governing role to a transitional authority after ruling Gaza for more than 20 years. It remains to be seen whether Hamas intends for this transition to be permanent — and whether a committee of technocrats can govern the coastal enclave while thousands of gunmen remain under Hamas’s command. Regardless, the 13 individuals profiled in this research memo have accepted the assignment to help implement President Donald Trump’s 20-point plan for peace in the troubled territory — and to rebuild it.
